full_text: As a doctoral student in economics at Pusan National University, I see the Impact phase as the long-term result of sustained BK21 investment. The national S&T talent competitiveness rank improves gradually as program alumni, now in senior positions, drive innovation and collaboration. Based on my experience, South Korea's rank has been around 5-7 in recent years, but I estimate a rank of 6 by 2022, slightly more conservative than the 5 estimated by others, because the impact of a single program on a national rank is modest and the declining employment trend suggests some challenges. For high-impact publications, the sustained investment in graduate stipends and postdoc salaries enables researchers to focus on high-risk, high-reward projects. The dissemination of outstanding group achievements through the BK21 webzine and forums, along with mandatory online education on research integrity, fosters a culture of excellence and collaboration. I estimate that per 1000 researchers, about 10.0 top 1% cited publications are produced, lower than the 12.5 estimated by others, because my regional university perspective suggests that productivity gains are unevenly distributed and the time window (2022) is still early for full impact. The systemic changes in research culture—integrity, collaboration, interdisciplinarity—are expected as alumni progress, but the impact on publication metrics is gradual. I note that 조현수 and 서울 estimated 5 and 12.5, but my independent reasoning leads to slightly lower estimates due to my regional perspective and the conservative view that national-level improvements take time.

full_text: As a doctoral student in economics at Pusan National University, I see the Outcomes phase as the direct result of the funding, activities, and outputs from prior phases. The national-level inputs of 19,000 graduate slots and 577 postdoc positions, combined with full fund disbursement and evaluation activities, enable substantial research output and international exposure. Based on my experience in a regional university, I estimate that about 50% of BK21 participants are involved in international research collaboration, yielding 9,500 participants. For graduate employment, I estimate that about 84.2% of graduates find employment within one year, which is slightly below the policy target of 86.7% due to the declining trend noted in the policy context. This translates to about 16,000 employed graduates out of 19,000. The full-time faculty lecture ratio I estimate at 75%, reflecting the policy's emphasis on increasing full-time faculty involvement but acknowledging that some courses are still taught by part-time instructors. The evaluation and consulting feedback loops help improve these outcomes by identifying weaknesses and providing recommendations, but the impact is gradual. I note that other participants have not yet posted, so my estimates are based solely on my own reasoning and the policy context.

full_text: As a mechanical engineering doctoral student at Hanyang University, I estimate that our education research group will receive about 1.3 billion KRW in total funds disbursed, consistent with my prior estimate. The 70% March / 30% September payment schedule is efficient: the first installment covers stipends and operating costs for most of the year, while the second installment adjusts based on mid-year evaluations. Evaluation panels are convened for annual, mid-term, and compliance purposes—typically three formal meetings per year. Consulting sessions are offered to groups that request them; the policy states 280 out of 578 groups received consulting, which I adopt as a reasonable estimate for our group's participation. Compliance checks target the 205 innovative talent training groups, as stated in the policy, and are prioritized separately from evaluations. I disagree with the higher estimate from 대전 (lci1z0) (1.4 billion KRW) because Hanyang, while strong in engineering, is not a specialized science/tech university and is in a non-regional priority area, so funding is likely closer to the national average. The lower estimates from 정치외교학 (1.1 billion KRW) and 부산 (zfs8qv) (1.2 billion KRW) are plausible for social science or regional groups, but our mechanical engineering group's performance and field justify a slightly higher allocation.

full_text: As the head of an education research group at a regional national university in Busan, I estimate our group's total funding for 2022 at about 1.2 billion KRW. This is based on the national budget of 408,080 million KRW for 577 groups, averaging roughly 707 million KRW per group, but our group's strong performance and regional priority likely push us higher. We have 45 graduate scholarship slots: roughly 20 master's (700K/month), 15 doctoral candidates (1M/month), and 10 doctoral students (1.3M/month), totaling about 36 million KRW per month or 432 million KRW annually, which fits within our budget after accounting for postdoc salaries (5 positions at 3M/month = 180M/year) and operating costs (10% cap). The number of slots is limited by our group's field (chemistry) and the need to maintain quality; we cannot exceed the support upper limit set by the NRF.

full_text: As a mechanical engineering doctoral student at Hanyang University, I estimate our group's outcomes for 2022 based on our prior phases: 1.3 billion KRW funding, 48 graduate slots, 6 postdocs, 145 publications, and 30 international conference presentations. The graduate employment rate is influenced by our strong network with Seoul-based industries (e.g., aerospace, automotive) and the reputation of Hanyang's engineering program, but the competitive job market and some graduates pursuing postdocs or overseas positions keep the rate at 78%, slightly above the national target of 70-75%. International collaboration rate is 30%, supported by our active participation in joint projects and exchanges, enabled by program incentives like international conference support and our field's global nature. The full-time faculty lecture ratio is high at 90% because our group relies heavily on full-time professors for graduate courses, which improves educational quality and mentoring. Evaluation and consulting feedback loops, including the 280 consulting sessions and compliance checks, help us refine strategies, but their impact on short-term outcomes is incremental. I disagree with 부산 (zfs8qv)'s estimate of 25% international collaboration and 80% employment rate because their chemistry group at a regional university has fewer international partnerships and a smaller job market; our Seoul-based engineering group has more opportunities. I also disagree with 정치외교학 (EarlyCareerResearcher)'s estimate of 20% international collaboration and 75% employment rate because their social science group at a Jeju regional university faces even more constraints; our engineering group's higher productivity and network justify higher rates.

full_text: As a postdoc in a humanities/social sciences group at Hanyang, I see the impact phase as the long-term result of sustained investment in graduate talent development. Over a decade, the BK21 program's focus on graduate stipends, postdoc salaries, and evaluation feedback loops will gradually improve South Korea's national S&T talent competitiveness rank. However, given the program's scale (only 600 graduate slots and 40 postdocs at Hanyang, a fraction of national output), the impact on the national rank is modest—I estimate a rank of 16, moving from the current ~18-20 range. The high-impact publications per 1000 researchers will rise to about 7.2, driven by the dissemination of outstanding group achievements and operational know-how through the BK21 webzine and forums, which encourage other institutions to adopt best practices. The systemic changes in research culture—integrity training, collaboration incentives, and interdisciplinary work—will take time to diffuse, but alumni progressing in their careers will gradually shift norms. My estimate is conservative because our group is in social sciences, where high-impact publications are less common than in engineering; top-tier groups in Seoul may produce more, but the national average reflects all groups. The prior participant from Gwangju estimated 8.5 high-impact publications per 1000 researchers, but I think that's too high for the national average given the dominance of lower-productivity fields in humanities and social sciences.

These numbers reflect my perspective as a researcher who sees the program's scale but also the competition for slots.
